evidence: Dependency direction is consistently outward-from-core: utils/model have no upward deps; query depends on model+utils; api depends on query+config+exploration+model; Main is the only entry point
No import cycles detected: model, utils, config are pure leaves; query imports model and utils; exploration imports query, model, config; api imports query, config, exploration, model

BlackLab integration is isolated in the query package (BlackLabQueryExecutor, CollocateQueryHelper, BlackLabSnippetParser) — the rest of the codebase depends on the QueryExecutor interface, not on BlackLab directly

com.sun.net.httpserver (internal JDK API) is used directly throughout the api package — HttpApiUtils, WordSketchApiServer, and all handler classes reference it; this is a soft platform-coupling smell but not a cycle
ExplorationResult.toMap() and ComparisonResult.toMap() embed JSON serialization knowledge inside model package classes — the model package has upward awareness of the API serialization concern
AdjectiveCollocateRanker is in the exploration package but is package-public — SemanticFieldExplorer wraps it to hide it from the api package (documented as intentional facade), so the boundary holds
RelationUtils is in the api package but only contains alias-to-config-ID mapping; this logic could belong in config or config-adjacent; its placement in api is a minor seam confusion

Package tree maps cleanly to domain capabilities: api (HTTP layer), config (grammar loading), exploration (semantic field analysis), indexer (corpus ingestion), model (domain types), query (corpus search), utils (shared primitives), viz (visualization)
CLAUDE.md and README.md accurately reflect the code structure: package listings, API endpoints, and command names all match runtime
ExplorationResult and ComparisonResult are documented as traditional classes (not records) with explicit rationale in Javadoc — ownership philosophy is documented

AdjectiveCollocateRanker was extracted from SemanticFieldExplorer 'to keep that class below the 400-line threshold' — this is a size-driven split, not a cohesion-driven split; the extractor is only used by SemanticFieldExplorer and is public rather than package-private
RelationUtils (in api package) contains alias-to-config-ID mapping that is logically config-domain knowledge; it belongs closer to GrammarConfigLoader or RelationConfig
PatternSubstitution (in api package) performs CQL token manipulation; its logic uses CqlUtils and is tightly coupled to the config/query domain, not the HTTP api domain
The viz package contains only RadialPlot.java — this is a thin package that makes the top-level structure look deeper than it is

The model package mixes result DTOs (WordSketchResult, CollocateResult), option bags (ExploreOptions), edge types (RelationEdgeType, Edge), and exploration model objects (DiscoveredNoun, CoreCollocate) — the package is a catch-all rather than a coherent domain boundary
All Java files follow standard camelCase for methods and PascalCase for classes — no snake_case outliers
Package names use underscore: pl.marcinmilkowski.word_sketch — non-standard for Java (convention is all-lowercase without underscores), but this is consistent across all packages
Handler classes follow a consistent pattern: ExplorationHandlers and SketchHandlers are both package-private, constructor-injected, and delegate to HttpApiUtils

Model types use two distinct strategies: records (DiscoveredNoun, CoreCollocate, AdjectiveProfile, Edge, ExploreOptions, QueryResults.*) and traditional classes (ExplorationResult, ComparisonResult) — rationale is documented in Javadoc for each traditional class

Constant naming is inconsistent across similar concepts: SemanticFieldExplorer uses 'NOUN_CQL_CONSTRAINT' while AdjectiveCollocateRanker uses 'ADJECTIVE_PATTERN' — both are CQL constraint strings but named with different conventions
Private record ExploreParams in ExplorationHandlers vs public record ExploreOptions in model package — both are option-bag records for the same exploration layer; naming diverges (Params vs Options)

BlackLab prefix appears in both package path (indexer/blacklab/) and class names (BlackLabQueryExecutor, BlackLabConllUIndexer, BlackLabSnippetParser) — the prefix is redundant in classes already within the blacklab subpackage but consistent with classes in the query package where BlackLab specifics must be distinguished
Accessor method prefix: ExplorationResult and ComparisonResult use 'get' prefix (getSeed(), getNouns()), while all record types omit the prefix — documented as intentional, but the asymmetry is real
BlackLabQueryExecutor consistently wraps InvalidQuery → IOException, preserving original cause: catch (InvalidQuery e) { throw new IOException('CQL parse error: ' + e.getMessage(), e); }
CollocateQueryHelper.getTotalFrequency wraps RuntimeException → IOException with context: throw new IOException('Unexpected failure retrieving frequency for lemma ...' + lemma, e)
WordSketchApiServer.wrapHandler provides uniform top-level catch(IOException) and catch(Exception) for all registered endpoints — prevents unhandled propagation reaching the HTTP server
ExplorationHandlers consistently separates IOException (→500) from IllegalArgumentException (→400) across all 4 handler methods

SketchHandlers.handleRelationsForType calls relationType.orElseThrow(() -> new NoSuchElementException(...)) inside the handler body — this NoSuchElementException is not caught by the method signature (throws IOException) and will be caught only by the generic catch(Exception) in wrapHandler, losing the specific error context in the response
handleVisualRadial uses catch(Exception e) broadly with no distinction between parse errors (4xx) and server errors (5xx) — all become HTTP 500
handleBcqlQueryPost distinguishes IOException (500) from Exception (400) but does so with a comment 'BCQL query error' that conflates parse errors with parameter validation errors

SketchHandlers.handleFullSketchForType and handleDepSketchForType accumulate per-relation errors in a List<String> and include them in the response rather than failing — good defensive pattern for multi-result aggregation

Main.requireNextArg calls System.exit(1) — acceptable for CLI but signals a mixed strategy (exceptions for runtime, System.exit for CLI argument errors) that is contextually appropriate but undocumented
ConlluConverter has a nested try-finally that correctly closes the writer even on exception, but the structure is complex and the resource-close pattern could use try-with-resources more uniformly

Domain-anchored names throughout: findCollocations, executeSurfacePattern, executeDependencyPattern, executeDependencyPatternWithPos, compareCollocateProfiles, exploreByPattern — all communicate intent precisely
buildBcqlWithLemmaSubstitution — verbose but accurate; the name explains the operation completely
extractLemmaWithFallback, extractPosFromMatch, trimLeftAtSentenceBoundary, trimRightXmlAtSentence — all reveal intent without implementation detail
OVER_FETCH_FACTOR in CollocateQueryHelper — communicates WHY the constant exists, not just its value

nounProfiles in SemanticFieldExplorer.exploreByPattern holds a Map<String, Map<String,Double>> of noun→{collocate→logDice} — but the variable is built as collocate→nouns and then inverted to noun→collocates; the name 'nounProfiles' is assigned to the final noun-keyed map which IS correct, but the intermediate variable in buildCollocateToNounsMap returns the noun-keyed map directly, making the name accurate
freqMap in BlackLabQueryExecutor.findCollocations is less specific than collocateFreqMap used in the same class's executeSurfacePattern — inconsistency for the same logical concept
wrapHandler in WordSketchApiServer — 'wrap' is generic; 'wrapWithErrorHandling' or 'withErrorBoundary' would be more precise
handleSketchRequest is a dispatcher that routes to sub-handlers; the name suggests a single operation but the body contains routing logic — slightly misleading
requireNextArg — clear and precise for CLI argument parsing
UNCOMPUTABLE in LogDiceCalculator — precise sentinel name, documents the exceptional state clearly
collocateFreqMap vs freqMap: same concept in different methods named inconsistently within CollocateQueryHelper and BlackLabQueryExecutor

QueryExecutor interface has 7 distinct methods (findCollocations, executeQuery, executeBcqlQuery, getTotalFrequency, executeSurfacePattern, executeDependencyPattern, executeDependencyPatternWithPos) — each method is distinct and the interface Javadoc carefully differentiates them; the interface pays for itself by enabling mock testing
CollocateQueryHelper is package-private and consolidates shared logic (executeCollocateSearch, buildAndRankCollocates) used by 4 methods in BlackLabQueryExecutor — genuine leverage

AdjectiveCollocateRanker is public but has only one caller (SemanticFieldExplorer); SemanticFieldExplorer.compareCollocateProfiles is documented as 'an intentional facade' over it — a single-implementation facade that adds an indirection layer without new behavior

PatternSubstitution has exactly one static method (substituteCollocate) with one caller (SketchHandlers.handleConcordanceExamples) — a single-caller static class, the method could be inlined or moved to CqlUtils
RelationUtils has exactly one static method (resolveRelationAlias) with two callers — borderline; the method does real work (alias mapping) but the class exists solely to host one method
HttpApiUtils has 6 static utility methods used across multiple handlers — genuine shared boundary, pays for itself
CqlUtils has 3 static methods (extractConstraintAttribute, escapeForRegex, splitCqlTokens) all used by multiple callers — legitimate shared utility
ExploreOptions record bundles 5 parameters to SemanticFieldExplorer.exploreByPattern — justified parameter consolidation; reduces method arity from 5 to 1
LongIntHashMap is a custom data structure with concrete performance rationale — not over-engineered abstraction

ExplorationResult.toMap() and ComparisonResult.toMap() are serialization methods embedded in domain model objects — leaky abstraction: callers (ExplorationHandlers) must know the map key names as implicit contracts

ExplorationHandlers.buildExploreResponseBody does unchecked @SuppressWarnings cast from Object to List<Map<String,Object>> — the abstraction boundary (toMap returning Object values) forces this unsafe downcast

The sealed ConcordanceResult interface (SnippetResult, KwicResult, CollocateResult) is declared but KwicResult appears unused in production paths — only SnippetResult and CollocateResult are constructed in query code

pom.xml production dependencies: blacklab-engine, blacklab-query-parser (corpus engine), fastjson2 (JSON), slf4j-api + logback-classic (logging) — 5 dependencies total, each serving a distinct purpose
No duplicate libraries for the same purpose: one JSON library (fastjson2), one logging facade (slf4j), one corpus engine (BlackLab)

blacklab-engine and blacklab-query-parser are both BlackLab modules — they are siblings from the same project, not duplication; both are required (engine for index I/O, query-parser for CQL/BCQL parsing)
com.sun.net.httpserver is used as the HTTP server — this is a JDK built-in, no external HTTP server dependency; this is a deliberate lightweight choice
slf4j-api 2.0.7 with logback-classic 1.5.18 — compatible versions; logback 1.5.x requires slf4j 2.x
JUnit 5 (junit-jupiter-api, junit-jupiter-engine, junit-platform-launcher) — test-scope only, three modules required for JUnit 5 standalone execution
fastjson2 is used both for JSON parsing (API request bodies) and JSON serialization (API responses) — consistent single-library use
No lock file visible (Maven does not produce one by default); version properties (${blacklab.version}, ${fastjson.version}) are used for version management, reducing drift risk

issues_preventing_higher_score: The only minor concern is that fastjson2 (com.alibaba) is a Chinese vendor library with occasional CVE history; for a local/research tool this is acceptable, but a production deployment might prefer jackson or gson which have broader enterprise security audit coverage
dependency_health.issues_preventing_higher_score: fastjson2 is a vendor library (com.alibaba) with less broad enterprise security coverage than jackson/gson alternatives; for a research tool this is acceptable but worth documenting as a deliberate choice

evidence: SemanticFieldExplorer.exploreByPattern is cleanly phased into 4 labelled helper methods (fetchSeedCollocates, buildCollocateToNounsMap, filterCandidates, identifyCoreCollocates) — each phase is clearly bounded and the top-level method reads as composition
LongIntHashMap has direct, clean linear-probing logic with well-scoped state mutation (keys/values arrays) and proper rehash
CollocateQueryHelper.collectHits processes hits in a single-pass loop with explicit null checks for each concordance field — precise edge-case handling
CqlUtils.splitCqlTokens uses depth-counting bracket walking with quoted-string skipping — the logic is non-trivial but is directly implemented without unnecessary abstraction layers; well-commented
AdjectiveCollocateRanker.buildAdjectiveProfileList accumulates stats (avg, max, min, variance) inline using Arrays.stream — direct and readable

ConlluConverter.convertConlluToWplChunks has a nested try-finally for writer close inside a try-with-resources for the reader — the inner finally correctly closes writer on exception, but the structure (try-finally inside try-catch inside try-with-resources) is hard to follow; try-with-resources could simplify it
ExplorationHandlers.handleSemanticFieldComparison calls result.buildEdges() twice: once to put in response map and once to get .size() — builds the list twice unnecessarily

handleRelationsForType contains a deep nested lambda with orElseThrow for an Optional inside a stream map inside a for-each — the throw site is buried and the exception type (NoSuchElementException) escapes the method contract silently

SketchHandlers.handleFullSketchForType and handleDepSketchForType share very similar structure (iterate relations, filter by type, execute pattern, build response) but are separate methods with duplicated boilerplate — the difference (surface vs dep) could be parameterized

BlackLabQueryExecutor.executeSurfacePattern computes collocateLabelPos before calling executeCollocateSearch but the result (collocateLabelPos) is only used after group iteration — minor ordering issue but control flow is clear

CollocateQueryHelper.scoreHits does Math.min(maxResults * OVER_FETCH_FACTOR, records.size()) with a comment 'safe: min ensures result <= maxResults*10' — the comment is slightly inaccurate (it ensures result ≤ maxResults*OVER_FETCH_FACTOR not 10), OVER_FETCH_FACTOR is 3
api→exploration boundary: ExplorationHandlers calls semanticFieldExplorer.exploreByPattern(seed, resolvedConfig, opts) with a typed ExploreOptions value object — clean minimal handoff
api→config boundary: resolveRelationConfig() and resolveExploreParams() are shared setup methods that centralize request-to-domain translation for all exploration handlers
RelationConfig.getFullPattern(lemma) abstracts pattern construction — the api layer does not need to know how BCQL patterns are assembled from grammar config

ExplorationResult.toMap() is called in buildExploreResponseBody which then casts result values: List<Map<String,Object>> seedCollocs = (List<Map<String,Object>>) resultMap.get('seed_collocates') — the seam requires callers to know internal map key names and perform unsafe downcasts; the translation boundary is not type-safe

handleSemanticFieldComparison builds its response inline (10+ response.put() calls) without using ExplorationResult.toMap() — two different serialization paths exist for semantically similar results, one delegating to model and one building inline in the handler

ExplorationHandlers.handleSemanticFieldExplore requires the 'seeds' query parameter (plural) but documents it as single-seed exploration — the parameter name does not match the semantics; the handler reads `params.get('seeds')` and treats the value as a single seed string
Data translation at boundaries: PosGroup is translated to string via getValue() in multiple handler response maps; there is no centralized PosGroup→String mapper

The model→JSON seam is partially in ExplorationResult.toMap(), partially in ExplorationHandlers.buildExploreResponseBody(), and partially in ExplorationHandlers.formatAdjectiveProfile() — three places share responsibility for the model-to-JSON translation, with no clear owner

Lifecycle propagation: QueryExecutor.close() is registered in a shutdown hook in Main — the lifecycle boundary is respected but the shutdown hook captures executor in a local variable which must be set before construction completes; the serverHolder[] workaround handles this but is non-obvious
Error propagation across the api→exploration boundary: IOException and IllegalArgumentException are explicitly caught and translated to HTTP status codes — clean and explicit at each handler site

CollocateProfileComparator has 12 consecutive logger.debug() calls including banner headers: '
=== SEMANTIC FIELD COMPARISON ===', '----...----' separator lines, section headers '
--- Building Comparison Profiles ---', '
Top SHARED (high commonality):', '

SketchHandlers.handleVisualRadial has 2 logger.debug() calls: 'Radial: body = {}' and 'Radial: center = {}' — restating inputs at entry, no diagnostic value

ExplorationHandlers.handleSemanticFieldExplore and handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti each have logger.info('Received request: {}', exchange.getRequestURI()) at the top — this exists in only 2 of the 4 exploration handlers and is absent from the sketch handlers; inconsistent entry logging with no observable pattern
No restating comments that echo the code — comments explain WHY, not WHAT; this is clean

CollocateQueryHelper.scoreHits has a comment 'safe: min ensures result <= maxResults*10' where 10 is the hard-coded value of OVER_FETCH_FACTOR in the comment — the constant itself is 3, not 10; this is a stale comment from when the constant was a different value

GrammarConfigLoader explicitly rejects the deprecated 'copulas' key and 'cql_pattern' field with an IOException: 'Config error: Grammar config must NOT contain copulas key' — old API is actively blocked, not just ignored

No @Deprecated annotations found on any method or class in the codebase

incomplete_migration.issues_preventing_higher_score: The 'legacy: tag=' comment in RelationConfig.posGroupFromConstraint documents an intentional backward-compatibility path for non-English grammars. While it is not a migration defect, it does mean two attribute-scanning code paths coexist (xpos= and tag=) with no clear removal plan. The comment 'keep this path as long as non-English grammars that use tag= are supported' provides no concrete milestone.

evidence: Package structure: api/ (6 files), config/ (4 files), exploration/ (2 files), indexer/blacklab/ (2 files), model/ (9 files), query/ (4 files), utils/ (3 files), viz/ (1 file), root Main.java

model/ contains 9 files: AdjectiveProfile, ComparisonResult, CoreCollocate, DiscoveredNoun, Edge, ExplorationResult, ExploreOptions, QueryResults, RelationEdgeType — borderline flat overload but all are genuinely model classes

QueryResults.java in model/ acts as a namespace container for inner records WordSketchResult and CollocateResult — this is an organizational choice that reduces file count but obscures discoverability

PatternSubstitution.java lives in api/ but implements logic that mirrors RelationConfig.mergeLemmaConstraint (in config/) — same algorithm for merging lemma constraints into CQL tokens exists in two packages

RelationUtils.java in api/ is a static alias table (8 entries) that maps API-facing short names to grammar-config IDs; it has no HTTP-specific logic yet lives in api/ rather than config/ or a standalone aliases/ package
LongIntHashMap in utils/ is used only by the indexer (not visible in any query or api import); its placement in utils/ is slightly misaligned with its only consumer (indexer/blacklab/)
Main.java is the single root-level file; it has high fan-in (it is the entry point) so it is correctly placed at root
viz/ package has only one file (RadialPlot.java), making it a thin-wrapper package with no growth visible

BlackLabSnippetParser uses informal /* @Nullable */ comments instead of a real nullability annotation (e.g., org.jspecify, javax.annotation.Nullable, or JetBrains @Nullable) for 5+ methods — these comments convey intent but provide no tooling support

ExplorationHandlers.handleSemanticFieldExplore: the URL parameter is named 'seeds' (plural) per HttpApiUtils.requireParam call, but the method Javadoc comment says '?seed=house' (singular). The variable is then named 'seed' locally. This diverges the documented API surface from the actual parsed parameter name.

QueryExecutor interface method executeQuery is documented as 'does not compute logDice or extract collocates' while executeBcqlQuery 'handles labeled capture groups and computes per-hit logDice scores' — the distinction is clear in Javadoc but the method names (executeQuery vs executeBcqlQuery) don't encode this difference visibly
GrammarConfigLoader.createDefaultEnglish() throws IllegalStateException (unchecked) but is typed as returning a value — callers have no static signal that this can fail at the call site
RelationConfig.collocateReversePattern() has a documented throws IllegalStateException but no @throws in Javadoc; however it is correctly thrown when preconditions are violated
ComparisonResult.buildEdges() Javadoc says 'performs list construction on every call' — accurate warning, but no caching hint or rename to createEdges() to signal side-effect-free construction cost
CollocateQueryHelper.extractCollocateLemma is a static utility (package-private) that falls back through two parsing strategies; this fallback chain is undocumented in the method contract
ExploreOptions record fields are not documented (no Javadoc on record components) — callers must infer meaning of nounsPerSeed vs topCollocates from context

CollocateQueryHelper.executeBcqlQuery: sampleSize calculation uses '(int) Math.min(hits.size(), (long) maxResults * 10)' with a comment 'safe: min ensures result <= maxResults*10'. The long cast is correct, but the resulting int could still overflow if maxResults is Integer.MAX_VALUE / 10. The defensive comment is accurate for realistic inputs but the cast is not completely safe for extreme values.

CollocateQueryHelper.scoreHits: limit = Math.min(maxResults * OVER_FETCH_FACTOR, records.size()). If maxResults is large, maxResults * OVER_FETCH_FACTOR can overflow int. records.size() is bounded by sampleSize which is bounded by maxResults*10, so OVER_FETCH_FACTOR (3) makes limit at most min(maxResults*3, maxResults*10) = maxResults*3 — but the overflow is still theoretically possible.

SemanticFieldExplorer.exploreByPattern: guard 'if (seed == null || seed.isEmpty())' appears AFTER 'seed = seed.toLowerCase().trim()' — NullPointerException would be thrown on null input before the guard is reached. However the guard comment says it checks after transformation, which means the null check is unreachable for null seeds.

ExplorationHandlers.handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti: 'int minShared = Math.min(exploreParams.minShared(), seeds.size())' — silently caps minShared without logging or telling the caller. This is intentional but could confuse callers who set minShared > seeds.size().

BlackLabQueryExecutor.buildBcqlWithLemmaSubstitution: throws IllegalArgumentException when pattern does not start with '['. The else branch says 'Unrecognized CQL pattern format'. Since all current callers always provide patterns starting with '[', the else branch is currently dead code.

SketchHandlers.handleRelationQueryForPattern: 'results.stream().mapToLong(QueryResults.WordSketchResult::frequency).sum()' is computed for 'total_matches' field — this is a valid summation, not a logic error.

GrammarConfigLoader.deriveTokenPosition: the cursor-advance logic uses both 'cursor += tokens.get(tokenIdx).length()' and 'while cursor < pattern.length && pattern.charAt(cursor) != [' cursor++' — this double-advance can skip characters when label prefixes are between tokens, potentially yielding wrong positions for complex patterns.

ExplorationHandlers.buildExploreResponseBody has @SuppressWarnings("unchecked") and casts Object from resultMap.get("seed_collocates") to List<Map<String, Object>> — the cast is from a stringly-typed Map<String, Object> return from ExplorationResult.toMap(), losing all static type guarantees

ExplorationResult.toMap(), ComparisonResult.toMap() return Map<String, Object> — serialization is performed via an untyped map, making it impossible to statically verify response structure or detect regressions when field names change

QueryResults inner records (WordSketchResult, CollocateResult) use String for POS ('pos' field) where PosGroup enum exists — raw string comparison or enum bypass possible when consumers check POS values

BlackLabSnippetParser uses /* @Nullable */ informal comments — see batch 13 — meaning nullability contracts are invisible to the type system; callers that dereference without null checks won't get compiler/IDE warnings
RelationConfig is a Java record with Optional<RelationType> field — good use of Optional for absence modeling
GrammarConfigLoader's parseRelationType returns Optional.empty() for unrecognized values rather than throwing — callers must use .orElse or .orElseThrow, enforced by Optional type

LongIntHashMap.get() returns 0 for absent keys (per Javadoc), which is indistinguishable from a stored 0 value — this is a type-system limitation of primitive specialization, but callers that need to distinguish absent from 0 must call containsKey first (this contract is documented)

SketchHandlers.handleVisualRadial uses JSONObject directly (fastjson2) with untyped getters; if 'width' or 'height' fields are missing, getIntValue returns 0, which is then checked: '(obj.get("width") == null) ? 840 : obj.getIntValue("width")' — correct but brittle

BlackLabQueryExecutor.java and CollocateQueryHelper.java share similar import blocks (both import HitGroups, Hits, HitGroup, BLSpanQuery, QueryInfo, etc.) — this is expected co-usage of the same library, not logic duplication

SketchHandlers.handleFullSketchForType and handleDepSketchForType both loop over relations, execute a pattern, collect collocations, and build a response map — their bodies share ~60% structural similarity but diverge in response fields (surface omits 'id','description','deprel'; dep includes them via buildRelationResponse). One branch uses an extracted helper (buildRelationResponse), the other builds inline — structural inconsistency in the same file
SketchHandlers has 7 private methods and 4 public handler methods in ~340 lines — coherent but borderline for a handler class
ExplorationHandlers has extracted resolveRelationConfig() and resolveExploreParams() helpers used by both single-seed and multi-seed paths — good DRY application
WordSketchApiServer.registerHandler() / registerGetHandler() / registerPostHandler() is a clean 3-level delegation — each method has a single clear responsibility

SemanticFieldExplorer has extracted 4 private phase-helpers (fetchSeedCollocates, buildCollocateToNounsMap, filterCandidates, identifyCoreCollocates) with sequential numbering and clear step comments — excellent coherence
CollocateQueryHelper has a clear 2-phase pipeline (collectHits -> scoreHits) with well-named inner record HitRecord to carry phase-1 results to phase-2 — coherent design
GrammarConfigLoader has parseRelations -> parseRelation -> validatePositions as a clear decomposition chain

BlackLabSnippetParser has 8 private static final Pattern fields compiled at class load time — all are immutable, thread-safe compiled regexes with no external dependencies; this is correct use of static initialization
RelationUtils.RELATION_ALIASES is a static final Map.of() — immutable at creation, no external dependencies, correct usage
SemanticFieldExplorer.NOUN_CQL_CONSTRAINT is a private static final String — correct constant
AdjectiveCollocateRanker.ADJECTIVE_PATTERN is a private static final String — correct constant
CollocateQueryHelper.OVER_FETCH_FACTOR is a private static final int — correct constant
BlackLabQueryExecutor constructor throws IOException when index cannot be opened — construction is explicit and failure is surfaced to the caller, not silently swallowed

WordSketchApiServer constructor takes executor and grammarConfig as explicit parameters — proper DI, no static singletons

GrammarConfigLoader.createDefaultEnglish() reads System.getProperty at call time — this IS an environment-variable read but it is method-scoped, not at class-load time; still couples config loading to JVM system properties

No circular initialization dependencies detected: the dependency graph is Main -> WordSketchApiServer -> {SketchHandlers, ExplorationHandlers} -> {QueryExecutor, GrammarConfigLoader, SemanticFieldExplorer}; all dependencies flow in one direction
initialization_coupling.issues_preventing_higher_score: GrammarConfigLoader.createDefaultEnglish() reads System.getProperty at call time and throws IllegalStateException (unchecked) if the file is absent. This is a test-fragile initialization pattern: unit tests that call this method without setting the system property will fail unpredictably depending on the working directory. The primary server path in Main.java avoids this by using the constructor directly, but the static factory exists as a latent coupling.

handleSemanticFieldExplore accepts 'seeds' parameter (plural) but semantically is a single-seed endpoint — misnamed compared to its counterpart handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti which legitimately uses seeds
HTTP API is synchronous (Java sun.net.httpserver, blocking handlers) — no mixed sync/async concerns

ExplorationHandlers.handleSemanticFieldComparison does not accept a 'relation' parameter (uses a hardcoded adjective lookup), while handleSemanticFieldExplore and handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti both require 'relation' — inconsistent parameter surface across semantically related endpoints
Server binds exclusively to InetAddress.getLoopbackAddress() (127.0.0.1) — all endpoints are inaccessible from any external network interface; this is the primary access control mechanism
HttpApiUtils.CORS_ALLOW_ORIGIN = 'http://localhost:3000' — hardcoded to localhost origin; any request from a non-localhost origin is rejected by the browser's CORS enforcement
All endpoints have consistent behavior: no authentication, no session, no per-endpoint access control — this is coherent null-auth, not inconsistent auth

HttpApiUtils has a comment: 'Restrict to specific origins in production. Change this value (or make it configurable) when deploying to a non-localhost environment.' — the production concern is documented
No admin/superuser bypass routes found — all endpoints have identical (absent) auth treatment
Input validation: query parameters are parsed and type-checked (requireParam, parseInt, parseDouble with error responses) — the 'trust boundary' for user input is consistent across all handlers

CQL/BCQL queries from user input are passed to BlackLab's parser — no injection sanitization beyond URL decoding; BlackLab's own parser handles this, but there is no application-level input sanitization for pattern values
No permission strings, role checks, or RBAC anywhere — consistent with the local-tool design intent
POST /api/bcql accepts arbitrary BCQL query strings from the request body — this endpoint could be a security concern if the server were ever exposed beyond localhost
authorization_consistency.issues_preventing_higher_score: The CORS origin and the server's loopback binding are the sole access controls. Both are hardcoded constants that cannot be configured without recompiling. If the tool is ever deployed on a shared machine, both would need to be changed, and there is no configuration abstraction to make this safe.

strengths: Strict layering: model and utils are pure leaves with no upward dependencies
QueryExecutor interface isolates the BlackLab dependency — switching corpus engines requires changing only the query package
Package-private visibility (CollocateQueryHelper, BlackLabSnippetParser, ExplorationHandlers, SketchHandlers) enforces internal encapsulation within packages
SemanticFieldExplorer acts as an intentional facade over AdjectiveCollocateRanker so api never reaches into exploration internals
cross_module_architecture.issue_character: Issues are isolated seam leaks — not cycles or hub instability — primarily model classes holding serialization knowledge that belongs in the api layer.
cross_module_architecture.score_rationale: The module graph is acyclic and direction-compliant; the dominant integration pattern (QueryExecutor interface) is well-executed. Two targeted leaks (model.toMap() carrying serialization logic, com.sun.net.httpserver permeating the api package) prevent a higher score. This is solid-but-uneven territory — around 80.

strengths: Top-level packages map consistently to domain capabilities; a new engineer can understand what each package does from its name alone
Documentation (CLAUDE.md, README.md) is accurate and in sync with the actual code structure
Public surface of each package is small: handler classes are package-private; only the explorer and API server are public-facing
The QueryExecutor interface cleanly defines the query subsystem's contract
high_level_elegance.issue_character: Issues are moderate boundary blurriness — helpers and utilities placed in the api package that belong to the config or query domain, and a model package that is slightly catch-all.
high_level_elegance.score_rationale: The high-level structure is understandable and doc-accurate, which is a genuine strength. However, RelationUtils and PatternSubstitution misplaced in api, and a model package that mixes too many concerns, create minor friction for someone trying to find or change domain logic. This is solid-but-uneven quality — a 74 reflects the good architectural intent undermined by a few placement decisions.

strengths: Consistent Java naming conventions (camelCase methods, PascalCase classes) throughout all 30+ source files
The two-strategy model type system (records vs traditional classes) is documented with explicit rationale, making it a convention rather than an inconsistency
Handler class structure is uniform across ExplorationHandlers and SketchHandlers
convention_outlier.issue_character: Issues are minor naming drift for closely related concepts — particularly constant names for CQL pattern strings and the Params/Options divergence — rather than structural outliers.
convention_outlier.score_rationale: The codebase is broadly convention-consistent; no file is a true style island. The identifiable drift (NOUN_CQL_CONSTRAINT vs ADJECTIVE_PATTERN, ExploreParams vs ExploreOptions) is localized and low-friction. This is solid quality with minor naming polish needed — a 77 reflects the slight friction without penalizing what is an otherwise clean convention landscape.

strengths: IOException wrapping is consistent throughout the query layer — InvalidQuery and RuntimeException are always wrapped with context before propagating
ExplorationHandlers has a clean two-tier error strategy (IOException→500, IllegalArgumentException→400) applied uniformly
wrapHandler in WordSketchApiServer provides a safety net so no raw exception can escape to the HTTP server
error_consistency.issue_character: Issues are a mix of broad catches masking failure modes (handleVisualRadial catching Exception→500) and one silent type mismatch (NoSuchElementException escaping the IOException contract in handleRelationsForType), suggesting the error strategy was designed carefully in most places but was not applied consistently in the handler layer.
error_consistency.score_rationale: The query and exploration layers have well-designed, consistent error handling. The api layer is uneven: some handlers are careful (ExplorationHandlers), others are broad or have escape paths (handleVisualRadial, handleRelationsForType). This is significant drag in a user-facing layer — a 63 reflects a strong core with a leaky outer boundary.

strengths: Domain-specific vocabulary is consistently used (logDice, collocate, lemma, deprel, BCQL, CQL) matching the computational linguistics domain
Constants like OVER_FETCH_FACTOR and UNCOMPUTABLE communicate intent beyond their literal values
Method names in the query layer (executeSurfacePattern, executeDependencyPatternWithPos) precisely describe what they do and how they differ from each other
naming_quality.issue_character: Issues are scattered minor naming inconsistencies — the same concept named differently in different methods — rather than systematic use of generic verbs or name/behavior mismatches.
naming_quality.score_rationale: The codebase has strong domain vocabulary and precise method naming in the core layers. The few inconsistencies (freqMap vs collocateFreqMap, wrapHandler being generic) are localized and low-impact. This is solidly above average naming quality — an 80 reflects genuinely good names with a few rough edges.

strengths: QueryExecutor interface is well-justified: distinct methods, clear contracts, enables mock testing
CollocateQueryHelper provides genuine leverage by consolidating 3 repeated search-and-rank pipelines
HttpApiUtils and CqlUtils are cohesive utility classes used by multiple callers
ExploreOptions record reduces method arity without hiding domain boundaries
abstraction_fitness.issue_character: Issues cluster around thin single-caller wrappers (PatternSubstitution, RelationUtils single methods) and a toMap()-based serialization contract that forces unsafe downcasts at the api boundary.
abstraction_fitness.score_rationale: The core abstractions (QueryExecutor, CollocateQueryHelper, HttpApiUtils) pay for themselves clearly. However, several thin single-caller classes add indirection without leverage, and the toMap()-driven serialization creates an implicit string-keyed contract that bypasses type safety. This places the codebase below solid — a 69 reflects real leverage in the right places undermined by unnecessary indirection elsewhere.

strengths: Minimal dependency footprint — 5 production dependencies, each with a clear and distinct purpose
No duplicate libraries: single JSON library, single logging stack, single corpus engine
JDK built-in HTTP server eliminates the need for a heavyweight server framework dependency
Version management via Maven properties (${blacklab.version}, ${fastjson.version}) centralizes version control
dependency_health.issue_character: Issues are absent at the structural level; the one concern is a vendor library selection (fastjson2) that is fit-for-purpose but carries mild long-term security audit risk.
dependency_health.score_rationale: The dependency set is exemplary in its leanness and purposefulness — no dead weight, no duplicates, no version conflicts visible. The single note (fastjson2 vendor choice) is a documentation concern rather than a structural defect. An 85 reflects near-exemplary dependency health with a documented minor concern.

strengths: SemanticFieldExplorer phased decomposition is clean and intention-revealing — each phase reads as a single bounded operation
LongIntHashMap is a direct, correct implementation of a performance data structure with well-scoped mutation
CqlUtils.splitCqlTokens handles edge cases (nested brackets, quoted strings) precisely without defensive sprawl
low_level_elegance.issue_character: Issues are scattered across: duplicated handler boilerplate (surface vs dep sketch methods), a nested resource management pattern that would be cleaner with try-with-resources, and one double-invocation of a list-building method.
low_level_elegance.score_rationale: The algorithmic core (exploration, CQL parsing, collocate scoring) is written with clear intent and proper decomposition. The api handler layer has structural duplication and one inelegant double-call. The resource management pattern in ConlluConverter is correct but unnecessarily complex. This is mid-range quality — a 67 reflects genuinely good core internals alongside avoidable handler layer duplication.

strengths: ExploreOptions value object cleanly bundles exploration parameters across the api→exploration boundary
resolveRelationConfig() and resolveExploreParams() centralize request parsing, keeping handler bodies focused on orchestration
RelationConfig.getFullPattern() hides BCQL pattern assembly from API handlers — good abstraction at the config boundary
Error-to-HTTP-status translation is explicit and consistent across ExplorationHandlers
mid_level_elegance.issue_character: Issues are concentrated at the model→JSON translation boundary: the responsibility is split across three locations (toMap, buildExploreResponseBody, formatAdjectiveProfile), one requires unsafe downcasts, and a parameter naming mismatch (seeds vs seed) creates a subtle API inconsistency.
mid_level_elegance.score_rationale: The inbound (request→domain) direction of handoffs is well-designed: value objects, centralized validation, clean interfaces. The outbound (domain→JSON response) direction is fragmented — unsafe casts, split translation responsibility, and two different serialization patterns for similar results. A 66 reflects good input-side seam design undermined by a messy output-side boundary.

strengths: Comments throughout the codebase explain non-obvious decisions (e.g., why ExplorationResult is a class not a record, why ExploreOptions uses 'Explore' not 'Exploration', OVER_FETCH_FACTOR rationale)
No generic domain names (handleData, processItem) in production code
Javadoc is present only where it adds value — trivial accessors are undocumented
ai_generated_debt.issue_character: Issues are concentrated in verbose entry/exit debug logging in CollocateProfileComparator and SemanticFieldExplorer that traces every algorithm step and uses ASCII banner separators — a hallmark of LLM-assisted development sessions where debug output was left in rather than converted to meaningful diagnostic logging.
ai_generated_debt.score_rationale: The codebase has clean comments and appropriate Javadoc. However, the exploration subsystem carries 20+ trace-level debug statements including ASCII banners and section headers that are the signature of iterative LLM-assisted development. Two handler methods also log request entry inconsistently. A 62 reflects meaningful technical debt in logging verbosity that would need a cleanup pass before the codebase is fully professional.

strengths: Completed migration fully: deprecated 'copulas' and 'cql_pattern' keys are actively rejected by GrammarConfigLoader with a clear error message, not silently ignored or shimmed
Codebase is a single-backend implementation (BlackLab); no coexistence of old and new backends
No @Deprecated annotations lingering on called methods; no stale migration TODOs anywhere in the seed files
The config version field ('version') is validated at load time, creating an explicit contract boundary that forces callers to acknowledge schema evolution
incomplete_migration.issue_character: The only migration-adjacent pattern is an intentional backward-compatibility branch for the legacy tag= attribute in non-English grammar configs, which has no removal milestone; this is a minor debt item rather than a structural migration defect.
incomplete_migration.score_rationale: The codebase scores near the top of this dimension: all identifiable migration work has been completed, old APIs are actively rejected rather than shimmed, and there are no stale @Deprecated callsites or mixed-paradigm coexistence. A small score deduction reflects the indefinite retention of the tag= legacy branch in RelationConfig without a documented removal condition, and a loose comment ('keep as long as supported') that could age poorly.

strengths: Core separation of concerns is clear: api/, query/, exploration/, config/, model/ each have distinct responsibilities
exploration/ was extracted from query/ at some point — the two-file package is small but reflects a real semantic boundary
config/ is cohesive: RelationConfig, GrammarConfigLoader, RelationType, PosGroup all belong together
indexer/blacklab/ uses a subdirectory for the BlackLab implementation, anticipating alternative indexers
package_organization.issue_character: The main organizational issues are boundary violations where logic that serves config concerns (alias resolution, pattern substitution) is placed in the api/ package, and a thin viz/ package that does not justify its own directory.
package_organization.score_rationale: The package layout is mostly navigable and coherent, landing it in the 'adequate but improvable' range. The main issues — RelationUtils and PatternSubstitution in api/ having config-domain concerns, QueryResults bundling two distinct result types under a namespace class, and LongIntHashMap being in utils/ while only used by indexer/ — are friction points that increase cognitive overhead when navigating the codebase but do not cause coupling failures.

strengths: QueryExecutor interface has thorough Javadoc distinguishing findCollocations, executeQuery, executeBcqlQuery, and executeSurfacePattern — each method's distinct responsibility is explicitly documented
RelationConfig.getFullPattern is well-documented with explicit statement of what happens when headword is null/blank
GrammarConfigLoader throws meaningful, specific exceptions (FileNotFoundException, IOException with descriptive messages) on misconfigured inputs
LogDiceCalculator.UNCOMPUTABLE sentinel and the note to use Double.isNaN() is a well-honoured contract: all callers (in CollocateQueryHelper) check for NaN as documented
contract_coherence.issue_character: The dominant pattern of issues is informal nullability annotations (/* @Nullable */ comments) that carry intent but no tooling support, combined with a concrete parameter naming mismatch between the API Javadoc and actual query-string key used at runtime.
contract_coherence.score_rationale: The codebase has strong Javadoc on critical interfaces and throws meaningful exceptions, putting it above baseline. Score is pulled down to 74 by the parameter name divergence in handleSemanticFieldExplore (seeds vs seed) — a runtime contract the Javadoc misrepresents — and the systemic use of comment-based nullable annotations that provide no enforcement. These issues affect API consumers and could cause silent 400 errors when clients follow the documented parameter name.

strengths: Guard clauses are used consistently throughout handlers (null check -> return early pattern)
LogDiceCalculator.compute uses Double.NaN as a sentinel for uncomputable scores with explicit documentation — clear contract
LongIntHashMap has clean, readable linear-probing logic with no obfuscated bit manipulation beyond the necessary hash index computation
CqlUtils.splitCqlTokens uses a well-structured depth-counting loop with quoted-string skipping — the logic matches its documentation
logic_clarity.issue_character: The main logic clarity defects are a null-after-mutate guard ordering problem in SemanticFieldExplorer (making the null check unreachable for null inputs) and an integer overflow hazard in two arithmetic expressions in CollocateQueryHelper.
logic_clarity.score_rationale: The codebase has generally clear control flow with few logic surprises; the 78 score reflects mostly clean logic pulled down by a reachable NPE in a guard clause, two integer arithmetic expressions that can overflow for pathological-but-technically-valid inputs, and a dead code branch in buildBcqlWithLemmaSubstitution whose existence implies the method's contract is more general than its current callers require.

strengths: Records are used for immutable value types throughout (WordSketchResult, CoreCollocate, AdjectiveProfile, ExploreOptions, CollocateSearch) — Java records enforce immutability by construction
Optional<RelationType> in RelationConfig forces callers to handle the absent case at the type level rather than relying on null checks
LogDiceCalculator uses Double.NaN sentinel with a documented constant (UNCOMPUTABLE) rather than a magic -1 or 0
QueryExecutor interface uses specific checked IOException throughout rather than RuntimeException — callers are forced to handle I/O failure
type_safety.issue_character: The dominant type-safety issue is the use of Map<String, Object> for all JSON serialization (toMap() pattern), which erases the type information that records provide and requires unsafe casts at deserialization boundaries; POS values stored as raw String where the PosGroup enum exists is a secondary bypass.
type_safety.score_rationale: Java's static type system means most type issues are caught at compile time; the score reflects the subset of safety concerns that remain — primarily the stringly-typed serialization pattern (Map<String, Object> losing record field types), one @SuppressWarnings("unchecked") cast, and POS values being passed as raw String rather than using the PosGroup enum. These represent real runtime-detectable type mismatches that would be caught by a typed serialization approach.

strengths: SemanticFieldExplorer uses explicitly numbered phase-helpers (Phase 1-4) with clear single responsibilities — exemplary decomposition
CollocateQueryHelper's 2-phase pipeline with the HitRecord inner record is a clean, coherent design for separating data collection from scoring
WordSketchApiServer uses a 3-level handler registration chain that cleanly separates routing, CORS/method enforcement, and error handling
ExplorationHandlers avoids parameter list explosion with the private ExploreParams record
design_coherence.issue_character: The primary design coherence issue is in SketchHandlers where two parallel dispatch paths (surface sketch and dependency sketch) share ~60% structure but one uses an extracted helper while the other builds inline — this asymmetry creates maintenance risk when the response shape needs to change.
design_coherence.score_rationale: Most of the codebase shows disciplined decomposition: well-named phases, extracted helpers, and clean delegation. The score of 73 reflects that the best patterns (SemanticFieldExplorer, CollocateQueryHelper) coexist with the less coherent SketchHandlers where structural asymmetry between handleFullSketchForType and handleDepSketchForType creates a maintenance hazard. The mechanical duplication signal (shared import boilerplate between BlackLabQueryExecutor and CollocateQueryHelper) is not a design issue — it's an expected consequence of both files working with the same library.

strengths: No mutable static fields anywhere in the production codebase — all static state is immutable (Pattern, String, Map.of(), int constants)
Explicit dependency injection throughout: WordSketchApiServer, SketchHandlers, ExplorationHandlers all receive their dependencies via constructor parameters
BlackLabQueryExecutor manages its BlackLabIndex lifecycle explicitly (open in constructor, close() in AutoCloseable) with no static ownership
Main.handleBlackLabServerCommand registers the shutdown hook only after all objects are constructed, avoiding a race between shutdown hook registration and object availability
initialization_coupling.issue_character: The only initialization coupling concern is the static factory GrammarConfigLoader.createDefaultEnglish() which reads System.getProperty at call time, making it test-fragile; all other initialization patterns are clean and explicit.
initialization_coupling.score_rationale: The codebase rates highly on this dimension: no module-level side effects, no singletons with creation-order dependencies, no import-time DB/network calls, and no circular init dependencies. The 14-point deduction reflects the createDefaultEnglish() static factory which uses a system property lookup at call time — a minor but real test-coupling issue — and the general pattern that GrammarConfigLoader can throw IllegalStateException (unchecked) from a factory method, which is harder to handle than a checked exception from a constructor.

strengths: 21 test classes with broad coverage of query, exploration, config, and utility logic
Unit tests exist for all utility classes (CqlUtilsTest, LogDiceCalculatorTest, LongIntHashMapTest) — pure-logic code is well-covered
BlackLabSnippetParserTest covers the XML parsing that is central to collocate extraction — high-value test for a brittle parsing layer
RelationsValidationTest provides schema-level validation of the grammar config, acting as an integration guard for configuration changes
test_strategy.issue_character: The indexer pipeline (BlackLabConllUIndexer + ConlluConverter) — which is the entry point for all corpus data and the source of index correctness — has zero test coverage, while all downstream query logic is tested; this is an inverted risk profile.
test_strategy.score_rationale: The test suite is strong for query, exploration, and config logic, but the zero coverage of the indexer pipeline is a critical gap: bugs in ConlluConverter or BlackLabConllUIndexer would silently produce a corrupt index, causing query results to be wrong without any test failure. This gap plus the absence of any end-to-end HTTP test pushes the score to 62 — well below what the quantity of test classes alone would suggest.

strengths: All handler methods follow a consistent pattern: parse params, validate, execute, build response map, send JSON — no mixed paradigms within the HTTP layer
QueryExecutor error contracts are consistent: all methods declare throws IOException for index-level failures
All HTTP endpoints consistently use sendJsonResponse/sendError from HttpApiUtils — no direct response construction outside the utility class
HttpApiUtils.requireParam provides a consistent 400-error pattern for all required parameters
api_surface_coherence.issue_character: The primary coherence issues are in the QueryExecutor interface where two pairs of methods have similar signatures but different semantics (findCollocations vs executeSurfacePattern; executeQuery vs executeBcqlQuery), and parameter ordering inconsistency between executeDependencyPattern and executeDependencyPatternWithPos.
api_surface_coherence.score_rationale: The HTTP API layer is fairly coherent (consistent response patterns, consistent error handling), but the QueryExecutor interface — which is the core programmatic API — has multiple coherence issues: name confusion between executeQuery/executeBcqlQuery, semantic distinction between findCollocations/executeSurfacePattern invisible from signatures, and a parameter ordering inconsistency in the dependency variants. These issues make the interface harder to use correctly and score the dimension at 65.

strengths: Loopback-only binding provides network-level access control that is uniform across all endpoints
CORS_ALLOW_ORIGIN is a single constant in HttpApiUtils — if changed, it applies everywhere consistently
No endpoint has more or less access control than any other — the authorization posture is perfectly consistent (uniformly absent, by design)
The production hardening note in HttpApiUtils acknowledges the localhost-only assumption explicitly
authorization_consistency.issue_character: Authorization is consistently absent — which is coherent and correct for a local research tool — but the hardcoded nature of both security controls (loopback binding and CORS origin) creates a deployment risk if the tool is ever moved to a shared or networked environment.
authorization_consistency.score_rationale: The codebase is a read-only local research tool with no user accounts, no data mutation, and no sensitive data — uniform absence of authorization is the correct and coherent choice. Score is 78 rather than higher because: the CORS origin and server bind address are hardcoded rather than configurable, and the /api/bcql endpoint that accepts arbitrary query strings could be risky if the tool were ever shared without the loopback constraint. These are forward-looking risks, not current inconsistencies.

issues.summary: ExplorationResult and ComparisonResult embed JSON serialization logic inside the model package
issues.suggestion: Move toMap() serialization to a dedicated mapper in the api package (e.g., ExplorationResultMapper), keeping model classes as pure data holders. This removes the implicit api-string-key contract from the model layer.
evidence: ExplorationResult.toMap() constructs a LinkedHashMap with keys 'seed_collocates', 'discovered_nouns', 'core_collocates', 'edges' and rounds logDice values to 2 decimal places — this is API serialization concern in a model class
ComparisonResult.toMap() similarly builds a JSON-ready map with 'adjectives', 'edges', 'nouns' keys
ExplorationHandlers.buildExploreResponseBody does an unchecked cast from resultMap.get('seed_collocates') to List<Map<String,Object>> — coupling api to model's serialization key names

issues.summary: com.sun.net.httpserver (internal JDK API) permeates the entire api package rather than being wrapped at a single point
issues.suggestion: Introduce a thin adapter interface (e.g., ApiRequest/ApiResponse) so handlers depend on a project-owned abstraction rather than the internal JDK API directly. Alternatively, accept this coupling if replacing the transport is not a realistic concern, and document the decision.
evidence: HttpExchange (com.sun.net.httpserver.HttpExchange) is a parameter in every handler method: handleSketchRequest(HttpExchange), handleSemanticFieldExplore(HttpExchange), etc.
WordSketchApiServer uses com.sun.net.httpserver.HttpServer directly in constructor and registerHandler
Replacing the HTTP transport layer would require touching every handler method signature

issues.summary: RelationUtils (relation alias mapping) is in the api package but belongs in config
issues.suggestion: Move RelationUtils into the config package (or fold alias resolution directly into GrammarConfigLoader.getRelation()) so that config knowledge lives in the config layer.
evidence: RelationUtils.RELATION_ALIASES maps short names ('adj_modifier', 'predicate', etc.) to grammar-config IDs ('noun_modifiers', 'noun_adj_predicates', etc.)
This knowledge is about the grammar config domain, not the HTTP api domain
GrammarConfigLoader.getRelation() is the natural neighbour for alias resolution

issues.summary: PatternSubstitution (CQL token manipulation) is in the api package but belongs in utils or config
issues.suggestion: Move PatternSubstitution into the utils package alongside CqlUtils, or merge its single method into CqlUtils as a static utility.
evidence: PatternSubstitution.substituteCollocate() calls CqlUtils.splitCqlTokens() and CqlUtils.extractConstraintAttribute() — it is logically an extension of CqlUtils
The class is package-private to api but its logic (CQL pattern transformation) is domain-neutral and reusable

issues.summary: AdjectiveCollocateRanker was extracted from SemanticFieldExplorer for size, not cohesion, creating an artificially split subsystem
issues.suggestion: Either make AdjectiveCollocateRanker package-private (since it has no external callers), or re-merge it into SemanticFieldExplorer and accept a longer class. A size heuristic is not a valid reason for a public type.
evidence: AdjectiveCollocateRanker Javadoc: 'Extracted from SemanticFieldExplorer to keep that class below the 400-line threshold'
AdjectiveCollocateRanker is public but only ever used by SemanticFieldExplorer; it has no independent callers
SemanticFieldExplorer.compareCollocateProfiles is documented as 'an intentional facade over AdjectiveCollocateRanker' — indicating the split is artificial

issues.summary: CQL constraint constants use inconsistent naming: NOUN_CQL_CONSTRAINT vs ADJECTIVE_PATTERN for the same concept
issues.suggestion: Standardize to either the _PATTERN or _CQL_CONSTRAINT suffix (prefer _PATTERN as it is shorter and already used in one place), or move both constants to a shared constants class in utils/config.
evidence: SemanticFieldExplorer: private static final String NOUN_CQL_CONSTRAINT = "[xpos=\"NN.*\"]"
AdjectiveCollocateRanker: private static final String ADJECTIVE_PATTERN = "[xpos=\"JJ.*\"]"
Both are CQL constraint strings used as the simplePattern argument to findCollocations(); the naming should reflect the same abstraction level

issues.summary: ExploreParams (private record in ExplorationHandlers) and ExploreOptions (public record in model) are sibling option-bag types with inconsistent naming
issues.suggestion: Rename ExploreParams to ExploreRequestParams (to emphasize it is HTTP-layer request parsing) or consolidate if the parameter sets are similar enough to share a type.
evidence: ExplorationHandlers: private record ExploreParams(int topCollocates, int minShared, double minLogDice, int nounsPerSeed) {}
model/ExploreOptions.java: public record ExploreOptions(int topCollocates, int nounsPerSeed, double minLogDice, int minShared, boolean includeExamples)
Both carry exploration tuning parameters; one is named Params, the other Options, with no documented rationale for the difference

issues.summary: handleRelationsForType throws NoSuchElementException via orElseThrow, which escapes the IOException contract and is caught only by the generic Exception handler
issues.suggestion: Replace orElseThrow(NoSuchElementException) with an explicit check: if the relationType is empty, log a warning and skip the relation (or throw IOException with a descriptive message). This makes the failure mode consistent with the rest of the handler layer.
evidence: handleRelationsForType (line ~83): obj.put('relation_type', rel.relationType().orElseThrow(() -> new java.util.NoSuchElementException('relationType absent for relation: ' + rel.id())).name())
The method signature is 'throws IOException' — NoSuchElementException is a RuntimeException that will propagate past the method and be caught by wrapHandler's catch(Exception) block
wrapHandler logs it as 'unexpected error' and returns HTTP 500 with 'Unexpected error: ...' — the client receives no actionable information about which relation was misconfigured

issues.summary: handleVisualRadial catches Exception broadly and always returns HTTP 500, masking client-side errors (e.g., malformed JSON body) that should return 400
issues.suggestion: Add a targeted catch for JSONException (and NullPointerException from missing required fields) before the generic catch, returning HTTP 400 with a descriptive message. Reserve HTTP 500 for actual rendering failures.
evidence: handleVisualRadial: } catch (Exception e) { logger.error('Error rendering radial', e); HttpApiUtils.sendError(exchange, 500, 'Failed to render radial: ' + e.getMessage()); }
JSON.parseObject(body) throws com.alibaba.fastjson2.JSONException for malformed input — this is a client error (400), not a server error (500)
obj.getString('center') returning null for a missing required field is silently handled but other missing fields could cause NPE inside RadialPlot.renderFromItems

issues.summary: buildBcqlWithLemmaSubstitution throws IllegalArgumentException which is not caught at the API boundary in handleFullSketchForType
issues.suggestion: In handleRelationQueryForPattern, catch IllegalArgumentException explicitly and return HTTP 400 ('Invalid pattern for relation: ...'). Alternatively, make buildBcqlWithLemmaSubstitution throw IOException so the error type is consistent with the rest of the query interface.
evidence: BlackLabQueryExecutor.buildBcqlWithLemmaSubstitution: throw new IllegalArgumentException('Unrecognized CQL pattern format: ' + cqlPattern)
handleFullSketchForType catches IOException | RuntimeException per relation — IllegalArgumentException IS a RuntimeException so it is caught here and added to relationErrors list

However handleRelationQueryForPattern catches only IOException — an IllegalArgumentException from executeSurfacePattern propagates past it to wrapHandler's generic catch, returning an unhelpful 'Unexpected error' 500

issues.summary: freqMap and collocateFreqMap name the same concept (collocate frequency map) inconsistently across methods in the same class
issues.suggestion: Standardize to collocateFreqMap (the more descriptive form) across all four locations.
evidence: BlackLabQueryExecutor.findCollocations: Map<String, Long> freqMap = new LinkedHashMap<>()
BlackLabQueryExecutor.executeSurfacePattern: Map<String, Long> freqMap = new HashMap<>()
BlackLabQueryExecutor.runDependencyQuery: Map<String, Long> freqMap = new HashMap<>()
CollocateQueryHelper.executeBcqlQuery: Map<String, Long> collocateFreqMap = new HashMap<>()
All four maps hold the same logical data: collocate lemma → co-occurrence count

issues.summary: wrapHandler is a generic verb that does not communicate its specific purpose (error boundary wrapping)
issues.suggestion: Rename to withErrorBoundary(handler, description) or wrapWithErrorHandling(handler, description) to communicate the specific cross-cutting concern.
evidence: private com.sun.net.httpserver.HttpHandler wrapHandler(com.sun.net.httpserver.HttpHandler handler, String description)
The method's sole purpose is to add uniform error handling; it catches IOException and Exception, logs them, and sends 500 responses
'wrap' could mean logging, retrying, timing, or any other cross-cutting concern

issues.summary: PatternSubstitution is a single-method static class with exactly one caller — the method should be inlined or moved to CqlUtils
issues.suggestion: Move substituteCollocate to CqlUtils as a static method, eliminating the single-purpose wrapper class.
evidence: PatternSubstitution.substituteCollocate() is called in exactly one place: SketchHandlers.handleConcordanceExamples
The method calls CqlUtils.splitCqlTokens() and CqlUtils.extractConstraintAttribute() — it is logically an extension of CqlUtils
The class adds one level of indirection without adding any policy, translation, or behavior beyond what the method itself provides

issues.summary: toMap() in ExplorationResult forces callers to use implicit string keys, causing unchecked casts at the api boundary
issues.suggestion: Replace toMap() with typed accessor methods (e.g., getSeedCollocatesAsMaps(), getDiscoveredNounsAsMaps()) or introduce a typed ExplorationResultView DTO that handlers can use without unchecked casts.

issues.summary: ConlluConverter uses a nested try-finally for writer close inside try-with-resources for reader — unnecessarily complex, could use try-with-resources throughout
issues.suggestion: Declare writer as a field or move it to a method that uses proper try-with-resources, or restructure using a helper that accepts the writer as a parameter. At minimum, add a comment explaining the nested structure's invariants.
evidence: The outer try-with-resources closes the BufferedReader; the inner try-catch wraps the main loop; the finally block closes writer via 'try (BufferedWriter toClose = writer)' which is a workaround for the variable not being declared in the try-with-resources header
The chunk++ inside the finally means the final chunk is counted regardless of whether an exception caused early termination — potentially inflating the chunk count in error cases

issues.summary: sampleSize and limit calculations in CollocateQueryHelper can overflow int for large maxResults

issues.summary: BlackLabConllUIndexer has no unit or integration test
